mnemonics.title: Entity Mapping Rules
mnemonics.content: REGULAR: Entity → Table; Weak: Owner PK + Partial Key = Composite PK; Multi-valued: Separate Table with FK

mnemonics.title: Relationship Mapping Cardinality
mnemonics.content: 1:1 - Choose one entity and add FK; 1:N - Add FK to 'many' side; M:N - Create new intersection table with two FKs

mnemonics.title: Attribute Handling
mnemonics.content: SIMPLE - Store directly; COMPOSITE - Flatten to components; MULTIVALUED - Separate table; DERIVED - Don't store, compute at runtime

acronyms.usage: Represents relationships between tables, references primary key of another table
Unique identifier for each tuple in a relation, cannot be NULL
Conceptual model used to design database structure before mapping to relational schema
Software system (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle) that manages relational databases
Relationship involving n entity types (n>2), mapped by creating intersection table with n foreign keys

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box with double border for weak entity. Show owner entity connected with double line. The weak entity table includes owner PK as FK, forming composite PK with its own partial key.
Think of intersection table as a 'bridge' between two entity tables. Both primary keys become foreign keys in the bridge, forming a composite primary key together.
Draw an arrow from 'one' side pointing to 'many' side. The 'many' side receives the FK representing the relationship. This is like a child inheriting parent's ID.
Think of it as a three-way junction. Three entity PKs come together in a new table. Each pair combination uniquely identifies a relationship instance.

analogies.analogy: A weak entity is like a child who cannot exist independently without parents. The child's identity partly comes from the parent (owner entity).
Foreign key is like an address pointer - it tells where to find the related information in another table, ensuring data consistency.
M:N relationship is like a marriage broker (intersection table) who connects two parties. Neither party can directly 'reach' the other without the broker.
Derived attributes are like calculator results - you don't memorize '15 x 3 = 45'; you compute it when needed. Similarly, compute Age from DateOfBirth when required.
